confidence.disclosure: Ibn Khaldun wrote his own autobiography, al-Ta'rif bi Ibn Khaldun wa Rihlatihi Gharban wa Sharqan ('My Journey West and East'), which is unusually detailed for a medieval Muslim scholar — including his appointments, dismissals, imprisonments, and the famous meeting with Timur outside Damascus in 1401. He even tells us his exact birth date. The retreat at Qalat ibn Salama (1375–78), the move to Cairo (1382), the loss of his family in the shipwreck off Alexandria (1384), the pilgrimage of 1387, and the negotiation with Timur (January–March 1401) are all dated. Year-precision throughout, and month-precision at several pivotal points.

sources: Ibn Khaldun, al-Ta'rif bi Ibn Khaldun wa Rihlatihi Gharban wa Sharqan (autobiography)
Walter Fischel, Ibn Khaldun in Egypt (1967)
Allen Fromherz, Ibn Khaldun: Life and Times (2010)
Robert Irwin, Ibn Khaldun: An Intellectual Biography (2018)

journey.event: Born on 27 May 1332 (1 Ramadan 732 AH) in Tunis into an aristocratic Andalusian family that had emigrated from Seville to Tunisia after the Reconquista. The Khaldun house still stands in the city.

The Black Death sweeps through Tunis. Both his parents and many of his teachers die. He calls the plague the great hinge of his generation: 'It was as if the voice of existence had called out to the world for oblivion and contraction, and the world responded.'
Takes his first government post at the Hafsid court of Tunis as writer of the official signature on documents — the lowest court appointment available, and one he later describes with embarrassment.
Leaves Tunis for Fez, the Marinid capital of Morocco — at the time the liveliest scholarly court in North Africa. Sultan Abu Inan Faris appoints him a writer of royal proclamations.
Imprisoned by Sultan Abu Inan after being suspected of plotting on behalf of a rival prince. Spends 22 months in jail, released only when the sultan dies in 1358.

Crosses to al-Andalus and arrives in Granada in December 1362. The young Nasrid sultan Muhammad V welcomes him; the vizier Ibn al-Khatib, the great historian-poet of Granada, becomes a close friend and intellectual rival.

Sent by Muhammad V on a diplomatic mission to Pedro the Cruel, king of Castile, in Seville — the city of his ancestors, now under Christian rule. Pedro is so impressed he offers to restore Ibn Khaldun's family estates if he will enter Castilian service. Ibn Khaldun politely declines.

Leaves Granada in February 1365 — relations with Ibn al-Khatib have soured — and crosses back to North Africa to take up the office of chamberlain (hajib) under the Hafsid emir of Bejaia. The post is the first that matches his ambitions.

His patron is killed in battle. Over the next ten years Ibn Khaldun changes employers and cities with disconcerting rapidity — Tlemcen, Biskra, Fez, Granada again — serving prime ministers and warring princes, leading punitive expeditions against Berber tribes, and once being robbed and stripped by nomads in the desert.

Tired of politics and intrigue, withdraws with his family to the remote castle of Qalat ibn Salama in western Algeria, the guests of an Arab tribe. He stays for over three years in 'a state of leisure that I had not enjoyed for a long time'.

In about five months of intense work composes the first draft of the Muqaddimah — the 'Introduction' to his world history that becomes one of the founding works of historiography, sociology and economics. He says the words came pouring out 'as if poured into me from on high'.

Returns to his native Tunis to access the libraries he needs to complete the historical volumes of the Kitab al-Ibar. The Hafsid sultan Abu al-Abbas accepts him back. But the imam of the Zaytuna Mosque, an old rival from his student days, grows jealous, and the sultan insists on keeping Ibn Khaldun at court.
Asks the sultan's permission to perform the hajj — the one excuse a Muslim ruler cannot deny. Sails from Tunis in October 1382 and arrives in Alexandria. He never returns to the Maghreb.
Travels on to Cairo and is overwhelmed by the city. 'He who has not seen it,' he writes, 'does not know the power of Islam.' The Mamluk sultan Barquq receives him with honour.

Appointed by Sultan Barquq as Grand Qadi of the Maliki school in Cairo and professor at the Qamhiyya madrasa. He works hard at judicial reform and quickly makes enemies among the entrenched court officials.
Disaster. The ship bringing his wife and children from Tunis to join him in Egypt sinks off the harbour of Alexandria. All are lost. Devastated, he resigns his judgeship and turns to teaching.
Performs the hajj he had used as his pretext for leaving Tunis five years earlier.

Returns to Cairo and concentrates on teaching at al-Azhar and the other great madrasas. He is reappointed Maliki qadi six times in all over the following years, always for short tenures and always making enemies.
Visits the holy places of Palestine, including Jerusalem and Hebron — a religious experience that he describes with unusual emotion in his autobiography.

Forced by the young Mamluk Sultan Faraj to accompany him to Damascus, which the Turco-Mongol conqueror Timur is besieging. Ibn Khaldun thinks the venture suicidal but cannot refuse. Faraj, hearing of a revolt back in Cairo, abandons his army and rushes home — leaving Ibn Khaldun and a few jurists in the besieged city.

Lowered over the city wall by ropes to negotiate with Timur in the Mongol camp. Spends seven weeks in the camp in a series of long conversations with the conqueror, who is fascinated by the lands of the Maghreb and asks Ibn Khaldun to write him a long report on them. Timur sacks Damascus anyway.
Returns to Cairo and immediately writes a description of Timur, including a sober assessment of his intentions, which he sends to the Marinid rulers in Fez as a warning.

Dies suddenly in Cairo on 17 March 1406 (25 Ramadan 808 AH), still serving as Maliki qadi. Buried in the Sufi cemetery outside Bab al-Nasr. He had never returned to Tunis but continued to wear, until the last, the dark burnous that is still the dress of North Africa.
